title: The Market
subtitle: A small light is still a light.

dialogue.text: The weekend market smells like cinnamon and old wood. Stalls stretch down the street in crooked rows. Music from somewhere. A man selling apples calls out prices nobody listens to.

dialogue.speaker: yen
dialogue.text: This place is HUGE.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Okay. Rules. Nobody wanders off alone. We stick together, we meet back at the fountain in one hour. Got it?

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: Yes, dad.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Ha ha. Here.
Drew pulls out some crumpled bills. Counts them carefully. Splits them.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Rush — here's enough for one thing. Choose wisely.

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: (Drew gave me his own money. He does that.)
Rush finds a woven bracelet at one of the stalls. Rose-colored thread. Lu's color. He buys it without thinking too hard.

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: Here. I saw this and thought of you.

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: ...You spent your money on me?

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: It's not a big deal.
It is, though. She puts it on immediately. Drew watches from across the stall. He doesn't say anything. He doesn't need to.
Rush finds a pocket knife with a wooden handle. Small. Practical. He likes the weight of it.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Good choice. Take care of it and it'll take care of you.
Rush puts the money in his pocket. Saves it. Doesn't know what for yet.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Smart. Patience is its own reward, Rush.
The stall is at the end of the row. Easy to miss. An old woman sits behind a table covered in lanterns — brass ones, glass ones, paper ones. All shapes. All sizes.

dialogue.speaker: yen
dialogue.text: Look at all the lights!
The old woman looks up. Her eyes find Drew immediately. She looks at him the way you look at something you recognize.

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: Drew, she's staring at you.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: She's probably just... yeah. That's a little weird.
Drew approaches the table anyway. He picks up a small, plain thing. Not a lantern exactly. A holder — iron, simple, with a groove for a wick.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: What's this one?
The old woman smiles. It's not a warm smile. It's a knowing one.
"A rushlight. People used them when they couldn't afford real candles. Animal fat and a reed wick. The poorest light there is."

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: How much?
She names a price. Drew pays without haggling. The others watch.

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: Drew, that thing is kind of ugly.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Yeah. But think about it. Somebody made this because they needed light and couldn't afford it. They used what they had. Animal fat. A reed. And they made it work.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: A small light is still a light.
He holds it up. Iron and fat and a reed. The ugliest light in the market. Drew looks at it like it's the only thing worth buying.
A black shape leaps onto the table. Kimiru. The old woman doesn't flinch.
She leans down to the cat. Whispers something. Her lips move but the words don't carry.

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: Is she... talking to the cat?

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: People talk to cats, Rush.
Kimiru listens. His ears flatten. Then he jumps down and walks away without looking back.
The old woman watches Drew walk away with the rushlight. She mouths two words. You can't read them. You want to.
Walking home. The sun is low. Drew carries the rushlight in his jacket pocket.

dialogue.speaker: yen
dialogue.text: My feet hurt.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Hop on.
He crouches. Yen climbs onto his back. This is a thing they do. This is a thing that happens all the time.

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: Drew, why did that woman look at you like that?

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: I don't know. Maybe I remind her of someone.
He says it lightly. But his hand goes to the pocket with the rushlight. And stays there.
Four kids walking home in the last light. The kind of evening that feels like it could last forever. It can't. Nothing can.
